original_abstract:
Despite decades of overhaul efforts and more than 150 major studies since World War II, the Department of Defense continues to struggle with persistent cost overruns, schedule delays, and performance shortfalls in its acquisition programs. This report examines the cyclical history of defense acquisition reform, analyzing why successive waves of legislative and executive initiatives have often yielded limited results. 

A central finding of this analysis is that past reforms have frequently prioritized regulatory compliance over workforce empowerment. Many analysts argue that lasting improvement depends on cultivating a high-quality acquisition workforce—ensuring personnel have the expertise, resources, and authority to make sound business decisions, backed by an incentive structure that rewards efficiency rather than rapid spending.

The current environment presents a unique window for meaningful change. A combination of budgetary constraints under the Budget Control Act of 2011, lessons learned from large-scale contracting in Iraq and Afghanistan, and the increased availability of empirical data has created a renewed appetite for reform. Recent DOD initiatives, such as the "Better Buying Power" series and the rewriting of the 5000-series regulations, signal a shift toward data-driven decision-making and tailored acquisition strategies. 

However, internal DOD policy changes can only go so far. This report identifies key issues for congressional oversight, including the need to further streamline complex acquisition laws, address long-standing workforce management challenges, and improve logistics and supply chain oversight. This report provides the necessary background and analysis to assist Congress in evaluating current DOD efforts and determining what additional legislative steps may be required to modernize the defense acquisition landscape.
humanized_abstract:
More than 50 years after a series of “transformation” efforts and hundreds of reports covering more than 150 studies since World War II, acquisition of defense systems is still marred by major cost growth, scheduling delays, and less-than-anticipated performances. Over the years, our research into the DoD’s acquisition processes has uncovered a cyclical history of attempts to reform defense acquisition practices that have been repeated, with generally little effect, over many years of different Congressional and Presidential reforms.

A number of earlier reports on civilian acquisition reforms have noted a tendency for those reforms to affect management of the workforce in ways that inhibit productivity. Such effects are now confirmed by analysis showing that civilian acquisition reform efforts in the past often tended to emphasize obedience to rules more than the autonomy of the people required to carry out acquisitions, such as purchasing and contract personnel. Further improvements will require development of an acquisition workforce able to make good economic and technological decisions regarding the purchase or development of goods and services for the government. Such personnel need the skills, the support tools, the authority to decide quickly and appropriately on actions required in an acquisition and appropriate monetary incentives tied to productivity such as paying for number of items purchased or the speed with which procurement decisions are made and transactions are performed.

We are currently in a window of opportunity to make real change happen. The impact of the Budget Control Act of 2011, lessons learned from the large-scale procurement efforts in Iraq and Afghanistan, and the availability of more data have all contributed to a heightened interest in acquisition reform. Efforts in the Department of Defense such as the "Better Buying Power" series and the rewrite of the 5000-series regulations are all indicators that things are beginning to change and acquisition is becoming a more data-drive and flexible process.

Regardless of the intentions behind the DOD’s current efforts to reform its acquisition systems from within, policies and regulations can be changed only up to a point. The report identifies acquisition system reforms that need Congressional oversight. These include streamlining what is a Byzantine acquisition process, fixing outdated personnel management practices dating back decades, and improving the auditing of the DOD’s logistics and procurement activities. This report assesses acquisition reform from a Congressional oversight perspective and is intended to provide the basic facts and analysis Congress needs to form a competent judgment about where the reform process currently stands and what legislative fixes may be required if the changes to current acquisition practices that have begun are to be sustained.
